en.extract:
The Acadian flycatcher (Empidonax virescens) is a small insect-eating bird of the tyrant flycatcher family.


Description
Adults have olive upperparts, darker on the wings and tail, with whitish underparts; they have a white eye ring, white wing bars and a wide bill. The breast is washed with olive. The upper part of the bill is dark; the lower part is yellowish. This bird's song is an explosive peet-sa. The call is a soft peet. They also have a call similar to that of the northern flicker.  A unique two-note song described as "ka-zeep" helps to identify this species.
Measurements:

Length: 5.5–5.9 inches (14–15 cm)
Weight: 0.4–0.5 ounces (11–14 g)
Wingspan: 8.7–9.1 inches (22–23 cm)


Taxonomy
Originally described in 1810 by ornithologist Alexander Wilson, its first scientific name was Muscicapa querula. The Acadian flycatcher is the only member of its clade within Empidonax, but is most closely related to willow and alder flycatchers.
Because of the similar appearance of the Empidonax flycatchers, the Acadian flycatcher owes its name to a misidentified type specimen for the species, taken in the historic Acadia region of Nova Scotia. Despite its common name, the Acadian flycatcher does not have a native range in Nova Scotia or historic Acadia.


Distribution
Its breeding habitat is deciduous forests, often near water, across the eastern United States and southwestern Ontario. These birds migrate through eastern Mexico and the Caribbean to southern Central America and the very northwest of South America in Colombia, western Venezuela, and Ecuador.
The numbers of these birds have declined somewhat in the southern parts of their range. Brown-headed cowbirds lay eggs in the nests of these birds in some areas. However, only 16% of cowbird young in Acadian flycatcher nests fledge successfully.
They wait on a perch in the middle of a tree and fly out to catch insects in flight (hawking), also sometimes picking insects from foliage while hovering (gleaning). They may eat some berries and seeds.
They make a loose, shallow nest in a horizontal forks of trees and shrubs, primarily in lowland forests and marshy areas.
The Acadian flycatcher is an excellent flier; it is extremely maneuverable, can hover, and can even fly backward. There is no scientific information on hopping or walking by this bird.
As of October 2015 there have been 2 records of Acadian flycatcher in Europe, the first being a bird found dead in Iceland in 1967, and the second a bird found on the beach at Dungeness in Kent, England in September 2015, the latter's identity being established by DNA from its droppings.


References


External links
Acadian flycatcher – Empidonax virescens Archived 2015-07-20 at the Wayback Machine USGS Patuxent Bird Identification InfoCenter
Acadian flycatcher Species Account – Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Acadian flycatcher videos on the Internet Bird Collection
Acadian flycatcher photo gallery Archived 2016-03-03 at the Wayback Machine VIREO Photo-High Res--(Close-up)

The '''Acadian flycatcher''' ('''''Empidonax virescens''''') is a small [[insect]]-eating [[bird]] of the [[tyrant flycatcher]] family.

== Description ==
Adults have olive upperparts, darker on the wings and tail, with whitish underparts; they have a white eye ring, white wing bars and a wide bill. The breast is washed with olive. The upper part of the bill is dark; the lower part is yellowish. This bird's song is an explosive ''peet-sa''. The call is a soft ''peet''. They also have a call similar to that of the [[northern flicker]].{{citation needed|date=May 2023}}  A unique two-note song described as "ka-zeep" helps to identify this species.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.sararegistry.gc.ca/species/speciesDetails_e.cfm?sid=19 |title=Species Profile (Acadian Flycatcher) |work=Species at Risk Public Registry |publisher=Government of Canada |access-date=19 June 2017 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170612221350/http://sararegistry.gc.ca/species/speciesDetails_e.cfm?sid=19 |archive-date=12 June 2017 }}</ref>

'''Measurements''':<ref>{{Cite web|title=Acadian Flycatcher Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology|url=https://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/Acadian_Flycatcher/id|access-date=2020-09-27|website=www.allaboutbirds.org|language=en}}</ref>

* '''Length''': {{convert|5.5|-|5.9|in|cm}}
* '''Weight''': {{convert|0.4|-|0.5|oz|g}}
* '''Wingspan''': {{convert|8.7|-|9.1|in|cm}}

== Taxonomy ==
Originally described in 1810 by ornithologist [[Alexander Wilson (ornithologist)|Alexander Wilson]], its first scientific name was ''Muscicapa querula''.<ref>Allen, M. C., M. M. Napoli, J. Sheehan, T. L. Master, P. Pyle, D. R. Whitehead, and T. Taylor (2020). Acadian Flycatcher (''Empidonax virescens''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (P. G. Rodewald, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.acafly.01</ref> The Acadian flycatcher is the only member of its clade within ''[[Empidonax]]'', but is most closely related to [[Willow flycatcher|willow]] and [[alder flycatcher]]s.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Johnson |first=N.K. |last2=Cicero |first2=C. |date=2002 |title=The role of ecologic diversification in sibling speciation of Empidonax flycatchers (Tyrannidae): multigene evidence from mtDNA |journal=Molecular Ecology |volume=11 |pages=2065–2081 |doi=10.1046/j.1365-294X.2002.01588.x}}</ref>

Because of the similar appearance of the ''Empidonax'' flycatchers, the Acadian flycatcher owes its name to a misidentified [[Type (biology)|type specimen]] for the species, taken in the historic [[Acadia]] region of [[Nova Scotia]].<ref>{{Cite book |last=Bent |first=Arthur C. |title=Life Histories of North American Flycatchers, Larks, Swallows, and their Allies |date=1942 |publisher=Smithsonian Institution |series=Bulletin 179 |location=Washington, D.C. |page=185}}</ref> Despite its common name, the Acadian flycatcher does not have a native range in Nova Scotia or historic Acadia.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Acadian Flycatcher: Minnesota Breeding Bird Atlas |url=https://mnbirdatlas.org/species/acadian-flycatcher/ |access-date=2025-07-17 |website=mnbirdatlas.org |language=en-US}}</ref>

== Distribution ==
Its breeding habitat is [[deciduous]] forests, often near water, across the [[eastern United States]] and southwestern [[Ontario]]. These birds [[bird migration|migrate]] through eastern [[Mexico]] and the [[Caribbean]] to southern [[Central America]] and the very northwest of [[South America]] in [[Colombia]], western [[Venezuela]], and [[Ecuador]].

The numbers of these birds have declined somewhat in the southern parts of their range. [[Brown-headed cowbird]]s lay [[Egg (biology)|egg]]s in the nests of these birds in some areas. However, only 16% of cowbird young in Acadian flycatcher nests fledge successfully.

They wait on a perch in the middle of a tree and fly out to catch insects in flight ([[Hawking (birds)|hawking]]), also sometimes picking insects from foliage while hovering ([[Gleaning (birds)|gleaning]]). They may eat some berries and seeds.

They make a loose, shallow nest in a horizontal forks of trees and shrubs, primarily in lowland forests and marshy areas.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Ford |first=E.R. |last2=Howell |first2=Arthur H. |date=1926 |title=Two comments on the nidification of the Acadian flycatcher |url=https://digitalcommons.usf.edu/wilson_bulletin/vol38/iss4/15 |journal=Wilson Bulletin |volume=38 |issue=4}}</ref>

The Acadian flycatcher is an excellent flier; it is extremely maneuverable, can hover, and can even fly backward. There is no scientific information on hopping or walking by this bird.<ref>Whitehead, Donald R. and Terry Taylor. (2002). [https://bna.birds.cornell.edu/bna/species/614 Acadian Flycatcher (Empidonax virescens)] ''The Birds of North America Online'' (A. Poole, Ed.). Ithaca: Cornell Lab of Ornithology; Retrieved from the Birds of North America Online (subscription required). {{doi|10.2173/bna.614}}</ref>

As of October 2015 there have been 2 records of Acadian flycatcher in Europe, the first being a bird found dead in Iceland in 1967,<ref>{{Cite web|title = MEGA – Acadian Flycatcher at Dungeness|url = http://thenaturequest.blogspot.co.uk/2015/09/mega-acadian-flycatcher-at-dungeness.html |publisher = The Nature Quest blog |date=22 September 2015 |access-date = 19 June 2017}}</ref> and the second a bird found on the beach at Dungeness in Kent, England in September 2015,<ref>{{Cite news|title = Rare North American bird arrives on British shores for the first time|url = https://www.telegraph.co.uk/news/earth/wildlife/11885236/Rare-North-American-bird-arrives-on-British-shores-for-the-first-time.html|newspaper = Daily Telegraph |date=23 September 2015|access-date = 19 June 2017}}</ref> the latter's identity being established by DNA from its droppings.<ref>{{Cite web|title = Acadian Flycatcher and Chestnut Bunting added to BOU's British list |url = https://www.birdguides.com/webzine/article.asp?a=6082 |date=12 December 2016|publisher= BirdGuides.com |access-date = 19 June 2017}}</ref>
